title: Combinatorics of the amplituhedron
speaker: Lauren Williams
affiliation: University of California, Berkeley; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: The tree amplituhedron $A(n,k,m)$ is the image in the Grassmannian $Gr(k,k+m)$ of the totally nonnegative part of $Gr(k,n)$, under a (map induced by a) linear map which is totally positive. It was introduced by Arkani-Hamed and Trnka in 2013 in order to give a geometric basis for the computation of scattering amplitudes in $N = 4$ supersymmetric Yang-Mills theory. In this talk I'll start with a gentle introduction to the amplituhedron, then give an equivalent "orthogonal" description of it.

proof_stages.label: Positive Grassmannian and positroid-cell apparatus
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Grassmannians via full-rank matrices and Plücker coordinates, defines the totally positive and totally nonnegative Grassmannians, and recalls Postnikov's positroid cell decomposition as the combinatorial source of cells.

proof_stages.label: Tree amplituhedron as a positive linear image
proof_stages.summary: Given a positive maximal-minor matrix Z, the tree amplituhedron is defined as the image of the totally nonnegative Grassmannian under the map A ↦ AZ^T, with basic special cases used to situate the object geometrically.

proof_stages.label: BCFW triangulation and topology questions
proof_stages.summary: Because the amplituhedron has dimension km, the speaker formulates the expectation that suitable km-dimensional positroid cells, especially the BCFW collections for m=4, should triangulate it, and motivates the accompanying question of whether its topology is ball-like.

proof_stages.label: Orthogonal-complement B-amplituhedron model
proof_stages.summary: For small m the amplituhedron is re-expressed as a space of m-planes inside W=rowspan(Z), namely B_{n,k,m}(W)={V^⊥∩W}, and this model is shown homeomorphic to the original amplituhedron by taking orthogonal complements and applying Z.

proof_stages.label: Sign-variation characterization and the m=1 equality
proof_stages.summary: Using sign-variation functions var and overline-var together with the Gantmacher-Krein/Schoenberg-Whitney total-positivity criteria, the speaker derives variation bounds for B_{n,k,m}(W) and explains why they become an exact description when m=1 via an extension lemma.

proof_stages.label: Sign-vector strata and m=1 triangulation candidates
proof_stages.summary: For the m=1 amplituhedron the speaker defines sign-vector sets with prescribed sign variation, partitions the amplituhedron into strata according to the sign vector of a representative, and relates this to a BCFW-like recursive triangulation.

proof_stages.label: Cyclic hyperplane-arrangement model and ball topology
proof_stages.summary: The final theorem identifies B_{n,k,1}(W) with the bounded complex of a cyclic hyperplane arrangement whose regions correspond to the sign strata, yielding the desired triangulation and ball-like topology in the m=1 case.

examples.name: Square-Z case: the nonnegative Grassmannian
examples.note: The special case where Z is square shows that the amplituhedron can simply recover the totally nonnegative Grassmannian, illustrating the definition rather than driving the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the square-Z case is only invoked with the statement that the amplituhedron is isomorphic to the totally nonnegative Grassmannian, without working through the case

passes.reason: The square condition k+m=n is a boundary case in which the amplituhedron is just the invertible image of the totally nonnegative Grassmannian.
The square condition k+m=n is the boundary/invertible-map case, so the amplituhedron collapses to the already-known totally nonnegative Grassmannian.
the square case k+m=n is a boundary case where the amplituhedron map is essentially an isomorphism to the nonnegative Grassmannian
square Z has k+m=n, a boundary parameter case where the amplituhedron map is an isomorphism to the totally nonnegative Grassmannian
the square condition k+m=n is a boundary case where the amplituhedron map becomes an isomorphism to the totally nonnegative Grassmannian
The square-Z specialization recovers the standard totally nonnegative Grassmannian, a systematic rich family rather than a collapsed, sporadic, or extremal object.
The square-Z case is the boundary case k+m=n where the amplituhedron map is invertible and recovers the totally nonnegative Grassmannian, not a counterexample.

examples.name: Projective polygon for k=1, m=2
examples.note: The speaker computes that the image of nonnegative projective coordinates is the cone spanned by the columns of Z, giving a polygon in projective two-space as a concrete special case of the amplituhedron.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly computes the k=1,m=2 map, sends e_i to the column z_i, and reasons that nonnegative coefficients give a cone/polygon in projective space

passes.reason: The k=1,m=2 image is an ordinary convex/projective polygon used as an illustrative specialization.

examples.name: Cyclic polytopes and the k=1 triangulation case
examples.note: For k=1 and arbitrary m the amplituhedron is identified with a cyclic polytope, and later the k=1 BCFW cells are mentioned as recovering Rambau's known triangulations, illustrating the general conjectural picture.
examples.engagement_reason: after mentioning cyclic polytopes, the speaker concretely identifies the k=1 BCFW cells by the sets {1,i,i+1,j,j+1} and explains that these triangulate the k=1 amplituhedron

passes.reason: Cyclic polytopes are structurally extremal because they maximize face numbers for fixed dimension and number of vertices.
The k=1 case is a base case, and cyclic polytopes are extremal neighborly polytopes maximizing faces for fixed vertices and dimension.
the k=1 base case gives cyclic polytopes, which are extremal by maximizing face numbers for fixed vertices and dimension

Cyclic polytopes are explicitly noted to maximize face numbers for fixed vertices and dimension, and the k=1 triangulation case is used positively rather than adversarially.
Cyclic polytopes form the standard extremal family maximizing face numbers for fixed dimension and vertices, and here serve as the known k=1 triangulation case.

examples.name: Cyclic hyperplane arrangement for n=5, k=2
examples.note: The drawn five-line arrangement and its region labels illustrate the theorem identifying the m=1 amplituhedron with the bounded complex of a cyclic hyperplane arrangement.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws the n=5,k=2 cyclic hyperplane arrangement and labels regions by tracking which hyperplanes are crossed, then relates the bounded complex to the amplituhedron

passes.reason: The cyclic hyperplane arrangement is the alternating/general-position configuration, attaining the maximal-region combinatorics for five lines.
The cyclic arrangement is the alternating/Vandermonde family dual to cyclic-polytope combinatorics, hence an extremal neighborly oriented-matroid type.
